title: FC-BGA (flip-chip BGA)
body: Die is flipped active-face down and joined to an organic package substrate by C4/Cu-pillar bumps; the substrate fans signals out to BGA solder balls on the board side. Bottom-to-top: PCB, BGA balls, substrate (with PTH/Cu vias), flip-chip bumps, die (underfilled), mold/lid. Must-label: substrate, BGA ball, flip-chip bump, underfill. Pitfall: do not draw wire bonds on a flip-chip package.

title: CSP / WLCSP (chip-scale package)
body: Chip-scale package substrate size does not exceed ~120% of the die (IPC/JEDEC J-STD-012). Wafer-level CSP (WLBGA/WLCSP) is built at wafer level with body size equal to the bare die; balls sit under the die via a thin RDL. Must-label: die, RDL, solder ball. Distinguish from fan-out, where balls extend BEYOND the die footprint.

title: Fan-out (FOWLP / InFO)
body: No silicon substrate: die is reconstituted in molding compound (EMC) and an RDL routes I/O out beyond the die footprint to solder balls. Variants: die-first face-up (InFO), die-first face-down (eWLB), chip-last (RDL-first, RDL built on a temporary glass carrier first). Must-label: RDL, EMC/mold, solder ball, no Si substrate. Pitfall: mold fills the sides of the die (die width_frac < 1).

title: RDL (redistribution layer)
body: Thin-film Cu wiring in polymer/dielectric that re-routes die pads to a wider or different pad array. Fan-out and interposers use 1-4 RDLs; finer line/space (L/S) enables denser routing. Represent as thin copper+dielectric layers, optionally with fine vias. Term: L/S = line width / spacing.

title: TSV (through-silicon via)
body: Vertical Cu via through a thinned silicon interposer or die enabling 2.5D/3D stacking. In a silicon interposer, TSVs carry signal/power from top microbumps to bottom C4 bumps. Draw as straight vias through a silicon layer. Distinguish from PTH (plated through-hole in organic PCB) and TGV (through-glass via).

title: CoWoS-S (2.5D on silicon interposer)
body: SoC and HBM sit side by side on a monolithic silicon interposer (with TSVs) which sits on an organic substrate. Three interconnect tiers, top to bottom decreasing pitch is wrong: bottom BGA largest, then C4 to substrate, microbumps to interposer smallest. Bottom-to-top: substrate (vias), C4 balls, Si interposer (TSV), microbumps (Cu pillar), dies [SoC, HBM...], mold. Must-label: Si interposer, TSV, C4 bump, microbump.

title: CoWoS-L (LSI bridge + RDL interposer)
body: Replaces the monolithic Si interposer with a reconstituted interposer: local silicon interconnect (LSI) bridge chiplets embedded in an RDL interposer handle dense die-to-die links, while global RDL/substrate carry the rest. Enables >3000 mm2 area and better yield than large monolithic interposers. Represent the interposer layer with an embedded LSI bridge (embeds, position top). Contrast with CoWoS-S in a compare.

title: EMIB (embedded bridge)
body: Intel approach: a silicon bridge is embedded in the top build-up of the organic substrate to link two dies with high-density routing, without a full interposer. Two dies straddle the buried bridge. Represent as a build-up layer with an embedded silicon block (position top) under microbumps, with the two dies above spanning it.

title: Hybrid bonding (SoIC / Foveros Direct)
body: Bumpless direct Cu-to-Cu bonding with a dielectric (SiCN/SiO) interface patterned with Cu pads at sub-10 um pitch, enabling 3D die stacking with lower resistance/inductance than microbumps. TSMC SoIC-X and Intel Foveros Direct use it. Represent as a thin bond interface (a bond row), NOT solder balls. Caption: bumpless Cu-Cu, <10 um pitch.

title: HBM (high-bandwidth memory)
body: A stack of 8-12 thinned DRAM dies on a base logic die, vertically connected by TSVs and microbumps; the base die holds the memory controller/PHY. HBM4 widens the interface to 2048-bit / 32 channels; HBM4E/5 move the first bond to wafer-to-wafer hybrid bonding. Represent as a base logic die (TSV) plus a repeated DRAM die-stack. Do not invent bandwidth numbers; mark as data-needed.

title: Backside power delivery (BSPDN / PowerVia)
body: Power routing is moved to the wafer backside, freeing frontside metal for signal and cutting IR drop; nano-TSVs feed power to the transistor layer. Intel PowerVia and TSMC A16 Super Power Rail are implementations paired with nanosheet transistors. Represent bottom-to-top: backside power metal, PDN + nano-TSV, device/transistor layer, frontside signal BEOL.

title: Glass core substrate + TGV
body: A glass core with through-glass vias (TGV, often hourglass profile) between symmetric build-up RDL layers; offers superior flatness, low dielectric loss, and CTE close to silicon for fine L/S. Panel format (CoPoS, ~310x310 mm) improves interposer utilization over round carriers. Bottom-to-top: build-up L1/L2, glass core (hourglass TGV), build-up L3/L4. Do not state exact via pitch unless given.

title: PCB stackup
body: Alternating copper signal/plane layers bonded by prepreg around an FR-4 core; through-holes (PTH), blind, and buried vias interconnect layers. Signal layers reference adjacent planes for controlled impedance. Represent as alternating copper (thin) and prepreg/core, core carrying vias, solder mask on outer layers. PTH is straight, not hourglass.

title: Solder joint shapes (drawing convention)
body: Match the drawn joint to the physical interconnect: round=generic; flat=post-reflow BGA ball (truncated, wider than tall); barrel=C4 flip-chip joint with concave waist between pads; pillar=Cu pillar with a solder cap (fine-pitch microbump); dome=hemisphere (bump-on-pad or encapsulation lens). Using the wrong shape misrepresents the joint type.

title: Underfill
body: Polymer dispensed under a flip-chip die to fill the bump gap and form edge fillets, redistributing thermomechanical stress and improving joint reliability. Drawn as a body filling the bump gap plus small fillets climbing the die sidewalls, resting on the layer below. Present on flip-chip dies; absent on wire-bond dies.

title: Mold / EMC
body: Epoxy molding compound encapsulates die(s) for mechanical and environmental protection; in fan-out it also forms the reconstituted wafer body. Usually drawn as a dark cap over the die (and filling die sides in fan-out). A lid/heat spreader may replace or top the mold in high-power packages.

title: Wire bond / QFN / leadframe
body: Legacy but common: die is attached face-up on a leadframe/die-pad and connected by fine gold/copper wire arcs from die-top pads to the leads, then encapsulated in mold. QFN exposes leads on the package bottom. Represent as leadframe, die (wirebond arcs), mold. This is NOT flip-chip; there are no bumps or underfill.

title: PoP (package-on-package)
body: A memory package stacks on top of a logic package, joined by peripheral solder balls; common in mobile SoC+DRAM. Represent as a lower package stack, PoP solder balls, then an upper package stack. Keep the two package bodies visually distinct.

title: TFT backplane
body: Thin-film transistor array on glass drives each display pixel. Bottom-gate (inverted-staggered) a-Si stack bottom-to-top: glass, gate metal (patterned), gate insulator (SiNx), semiconductor channel (a-Si/IGZO), source/drain metal, passivation, pixel electrode (ITO). Layers are patterned (width_frac < 1). IGZO/LTPS are higher-mobility alternatives to a-Si.

title: OLED stack + TFE
body: Organic emissive stack on a TFT backplane: anode (ITO), hole injection/transport (HIL/HTL), emission layer (EML), electron transport/injection (ETL/EIL), cathode (Mg:Ag, thin/semitransparent for top emission), then thin-film encapsulation (TFE) as inorganic/organic/inorganic layers protecting against moisture and oxygen. Emissive organics are sensitive to water/oxygen; TFE is essential.

title: Micro-LED chip
body: Inorganic GaN LED (<50 um) grown on sapphire: sapphire, n-GaN, MQW active region (narrower), p-GaN, p-electrode (ITO). Canonical color pair: n-GaN blue, p-GaN magenta. Chips are singulated and mass-transferred to a TFT/driver backplane (laser lift-off, stamp transfer). Distinguish from OLED (inorganic vs organic emission).

title: Mini-LED backlight (BLU)
body: Direct-lit LCD backlight using a dense array of ~100-200 um mini-LEDs grouped into local-dimming zones for HDR contrast. Bottom-to-top: BLU PCB, mini-LED chips, diffuser, optional QD film, prism/BEF films, LCD cell. Mini-LED lights an LCD; it does not emit the image itself (unlike micro-LED).

title: Terminology and units discipline
body: Keep one canonical spelling per term (microbump, C4, TSV, TGV, RDL, PTH, L/S, BEOL). Use SI units with a space (100 um, 45 um pitch). Prefer micrometre symbol um in ASCII contexts. Do not conflate 2.5D (side-by-side on interposer) with 3D (vertically stacked dies). Do not invent dimensions; if needed and unknown, mark data-needed.

title: 2.5D vs 3D integration
body: 2.5D places multiple dies side by side on an interposer/bridge (CoWoS, EMIB); 3D stacks dies vertically with TSVs and microbumps or hybrid bonding (Foveros, SoIC, HBM). Heterogeneous integration combines chiplets of different nodes/functions in one package. Use the right framing: interposer/bridge for 2.5D, vertical stack/bond for 3D.

title: Chiplets and die-to-die interfaces
body: A chiplet is a smaller die integrated with others in one package to improve yield and mix process nodes. Die-to-die links use standardized interfaces (e.g., UCIe) over bridges, interposers, or hybrid bonds. In diagrams, show chiplets as multiple dies with an explicit interconnect medium (bridge/interposer/bond), not floating.

title: Thermal and reliability context
body: Advanced packages add thermal paths (lids, heat spreaders, TIM) and face CTE-mismatch stress managed by underfill and material selection. Backside power and dense 3D stacks intensify thermal density. When a slide claims a benefit (bandwidth, density), the honest counterpoint is often thermal or cost; note it if the deck argues a tradeoff.

title: Organic package substrate (ABF build-up)
body: Organic substrates use an core with symmetric build-up layers of ABF (Ajinomoto Build-up Film) dielectric and patterned Cu, with laser vias between build-up layers and PTH through the core. Glass-core substrates are an emerging replacement for the organic core. Drawn as tan dielectric build-up around a core, Cu vias through.

title: Cross-section scale honesty
body: Real packages span orders of magnitude in thickness (mm boards to sub-um films), so cross-sections exaggerate thin layers for visibility. Always caption not-to-scale when doing so, keep relative ordering physically correct, and avoid implying a false pitch or aspect ratio in comparisons.

title: Silicon photonics: what's on the chip
body: A silicon/SiN photonic IC (PIC) integrates optical elements joined by IN-PLANE waveguides — the story is a horizontal optical path, not a vertical layer stack (use the 'photonic' figure, not 'stack'). Core elements and roles: LIGHT SOURCE = laser (DFB, often flip-chip or edge-attached; a comb/array laser for WDM). COUPLER (light in/out) = grating coupler (vertical, fiber sits above) or edge coupler + spot-size converter (SSC) with a fiber array (V-groove). MODULATOR (imprints data on light) = Mach-Zehnder modulator (MZM) or microring modulator. (DE)MUX for WDM = arrayed-waveguide grating (AWG) or cascaded ring filters. DETECTOR = Ge photodiode (or APD). ELECTRONICS = EIC with driver (drives modulator) and TIA (amplifies PD current). PASSIVES = splitters (1xN MMI/directional coupler), phase shifters (thermo-optic heaters).

title: Photonic transmitter arrangement
body: Canonical TX optical path (left to right): laser -> (1xN splitter for WDM) -> modulator per wavelength (MZM or ring, each driven by an electronic DRIVER) -> WDM MUX (AWG or ring cascade) -> grating/edge coupler -> fiber. The drivers are electronic ICs placed BELOW the modulators and connected electrically. Must-label: laser, modulator (MZM/ring), MUX, coupler, fiber. Pitfall: the laser is the source (emits); do not put a photodiode on the transmit path.

title: Photonic receiver arrangement
body: Canonical RX optical path: fiber -> grating/edge coupler -> WDM DEMUX (AWG or ring drop filters) -> Ge photodiode per wavelength -> TIA (transimpedance amplifier, electronic). Each photodiode DETECTS light and feeds a TIA placed below it. Must-label: coupler, DEMUX, photodiode, TIA. A ring-based receiver uses one microring drop filter per wavelength tapping a shared bus waveguide, each dropping to its own PD.

title: Fiber coupling: grating vs edge
body: Two ways to get light between fiber and chip. GRATING COUPLER: light couples vertically (fiber mounted above the chip surface at ~8-10 deg); wafer-level testable, wavelength/polarization sensitive; drawn as a comb of grating lines. EDGE COUPLER: light couples in-plane at the chip edge via an inverse taper / spot-size converter (SSC) to a fiber array (V-groove); low loss and broadband; drawn as a taper. Fiber attach uses a fiber array unit (FAU). Must-label the coupler type.

title: Modulators: MZM vs microring
body: MACH-ZEHNDER MODULATOR (MZM): splits light into two arms, applies a phase shift, recombines to interfere — broadband, larger footprint, tolerant. MICRORING MODULATOR: a resonant ring coupled to a bus waveguide — very compact and low-power, but temperature-sensitive (needs a heater). Both need an electronic DRIVER. Draw MZM as an interferometer (two arms), a ring as a circle beside the bus.

title: WDM (de)multiplexers
body: To pack multiple wavelengths on one fiber: MUX combines, DEMUX separates. ARRAYED WAVEGUIDE GRATING (AWG): a slab + waveguide array, many channels, drawn as a trapezoidal slab. RING FILTER CASCADE: a series of microrings each resonant at one wavelength, tapping a shared bus. Must-label channel wavelengths (λ1, λ2, ...) if shown; do not invent exact nm values unless given.

title: Co-packaged optics (CPO)
body: CPO places the optical engine (PIC + EIC) next to a switch/XPU ASIC on a shared package substrate to cut electrical reach. Layout: central switch ASIC surrounded by optical engines; each engine = PIC (modulators, couplers) + EIC (drivers, TIAs, SerDes) integrated 2.5D (side-by-side on interposer) or 3D (EIC stacked on PIC via microbumps/hybrid bond); fibers exit the package edge. The PIC+EIC vertical integration itself is a layer STACK; the optical routing is a photonic graph.

title: Glass / polymer-waveguide photonic interposer
body: A glass substrate can serve as a photonic interposer: Cu RDL and pads on the surface for electrical routing, PLUS polymer (or ion-exchange) waveguides for in-plane optical routing. Parts (laser, driver IC, passives, photodiode) are surface-mounted; the laser edge-couples into the polymer waveguide and light travels laterally to the photodiode. Electrical: laser driver and TIA connect via the Cu RDL. Draw as a photonic figure with the waveguide in-plane (edge-coupled), not a vertical stack.

title: PIC + EIC electronic integration
body: The photonic IC (PIC) carries no gain/logic — an electronic IC (EIC) drives it. DRIVER converts electrical data to the modulator's drive signal; TIA converts photodiode current to voltage; plus CDR/SerDes/controller. Integration: 2.5D (PIC and EIC side-by-side on a Si/glass/organic interposer) or 3D (EIC flip-chip or hybrid-bonded on top of the PIC). In a schematic, place electronic blocks (driver/TIA) below their optical partners (modulator/PD) and connect with electrical lines.

title: Photonic diagram pitfalls
body: Do NOT force a photonic IC into a vertical layer stack — the essential content is the lateral optical path (use the 'photonic' figure). Distinguish optical links (waveguides carrying light, drawn as a light path with an arrow) from electrical links (driver/TIA wiring). The laser is the only source (emits); the photodiode is the only detector (receives). Modulators and rings do not generate light — they need upstream laser light. Rings and MZMs need a driver; rings usually need a heater for thermal tuning.
